title: The Best Ground Covers for Soil Protection
excerpt: Ground covers help protect soil by reducing erosion, lowering evaporation, suppressing weeds, and keeping living roots active near the surface. The best ground covers for soil protection are the ones that suit your climate, planting style, and maintenance goals while keeping the soil covered and stable.
author: Permatable Team
content:
Ground Covers for Soil Health

One of the best ways to protect soil is to stop leaving it exposed. Ground covers help do that by forming a living layer over the soil surface. They reduce erosion, hold moisture longer, lower weed pressure, and support the living system below. In many gardens, they can be one of the most effective long-term tools for protecting soil naturally.

Unlike bare ground, living ground cover continues working every day. It shades the soil, keeps roots active, and often improves the whole garden system over time. The key is choosing the right kind of cover for the space, the climate, and the role you want it to play.

Why Ground Covers Matter for Soil Protection

Soil is most vulnerable when it is bare.

Exposed ground is more likely to:

  • dry out quickly
  • erode in rain or wind
  • form a hard crust
  • lose organic matter faster
  • fill with opportunistic weeds

Ground covers help prevent these problems by keeping a living layer over the surface and adding root activity below it.

What Makes a Good Ground Cover

The best ground covers for soil protection are not always the same plants in every garden. The right choice depends on your conditions and goals.

In general, a good ground cover should:

  • cover the soil well
  • hold the surface with roots
  • fit your climate
  • avoid becoming a bigger problem than it solves
  • work with nearby crops or ornamentals

The most useful ground covers protect the soil without overwhelming the rest of the garden.

Ground Covers Reduce Moisture Loss

One of their biggest strengths is helping the soil hold moisture longer.

They do this by:

  • shading the surface
  • reducing evaporation
  • buffering heat and wind
  • keeping the topsoil cooler

In hot or dry conditions, this can make a noticeable difference in how often watering is needed.

They Help Prevent Erosion

Ground covers protect soil from the impact of heavy rain and from movement caused by runoff or wind.

They help by:

  • holding the soil with roots
  • slowing water across the surface
  • reducing exposed loose ground
  • creating a more stable upper layer

This makes them especially useful on slopes, exposed borders, and areas where mulch alone may shift or thin out over time.

They Suppress Weeds Naturally

A dense ground cover can reduce the space and light available to weeds.

This often helps by:

  • occupying open soil before weeds move in
  • reducing repeated disturbance from weeding
  • creating a more stable surface layer

While ground covers do not eliminate all weeds, they can make weed pressure much easier to manage.

They Keep Roots Active Near the Surface

Living roots help support microbes, improve soil aggregation, and keep the upper soil layers more biologically active.

That means ground covers protect the soil not only from above, but also from within the soil itself through root activity.

This makes them different from mulch alone, even though both are useful.

Low-Growing Clover Types

Low-growing clovers are often valued as soil-protective ground covers because they can:

  • cover open ground well
  • support soil health
  • reduce erosion
  • fit into mixed garden systems in some situations

They are especially useful where a soft living cover is wanted rather than a purely ornamental planting.

Strawberries as a Productive Ground Cover

Strawberries can work well as a ground cover in some edible gardens because they protect the soil while also producing a harvest.

They help by:

  • spreading across the surface
  • reducing exposed ground
  • shading the root zone
  • adding a productive layer to the garden

They are especially useful in beds where you want a living mulch effect with food value included.

Low Herbs for Soil Protection

Certain low-growing herbs can make useful ground covers, especially in beds that combine beauty, pollinator support, and soil care.

Depending on climate and placement, these may help by:

  • covering open spaces
  • supporting beneficial insects
  • reducing moisture loss
  • making the bed more diverse and resilient

This is a good option in herb gardens, mixed borders, and permaculture-style plantings.

Native Ground Covers

Native ground covers are often some of the best long-term options because they are already adapted to local conditions.

They may offer:

  • better climate fit
  • support for local wildlife
  • lower maintenance once established
  • stronger resilience under local weather conditions

If your goal is both soil protection and ecological support, native options are often worth prioritizing.

Ground Covers for Shade

Shaded areas often need different kinds of ground cover than hot sunny beds.

In those spaces, useful ground covers are usually those that can:

  • fill under trees or shrubs
  • protect the surface from drying and erosion
  • handle lower light well

Shady ground that is left bare often degrades quickly, so the right cover can be especially valuable there.

Ground Covers for Slopes

Sloped ground needs strong root holding power and reliable coverage.

The best ground covers for slopes are usually those that:

  • establish well
  • spread enough to hold exposed soil
  • reduce runoff
  • stay stable in varying weather

On slopes, soil protection is often the top priority, so plant choice should focus heavily on stability and coverage.

Living Mulch in Vegetable Gardens

In some vegetable gardens, ground covers are used as living mulch between crops or around larger plants.

This can help:

  • reduce bare soil
  • hold moisture
  • support soil biology
  • reduce weed pressure

However, living mulches need to be chosen carefully so they do not compete too heavily with the main crop.

Ground Covers Work Best With Good Placement

Even the best ground cover can become a problem if it is placed without thought.

Before choosing one, ask:

  • How aggressive is it?
  • Will it compete with nearby crops?
  • Does it suit the light and moisture conditions?
  • Will it be easy enough to manage?

The goal is soil protection, not creating a new maintenance problem.

Ground Covers and Mulch Can Work Together

Ground covers do not always replace mulch. In many gardens, the two can complement each other.

For example:

  • mulch may protect the soil while the ground cover establishes
  • ground covers may fill gaps where mulch breaks down or shifts
  • both can support moisture retention and lower weed pressure

Used together, they often provide stronger protection than either one alone.

What to Avoid

Not every spreading plant is a good ground cover.

Plants that spread too aggressively or become invasive in your area can create more problems than they solve. Others may compete too heavily with crops or require more control than expected.

The best ground covers are protective, useful, and manageable in your specific setting.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

A few issues often make ground covers less effective than they should be.

  • Choosing plants only because they spread quickly: speed without control can become a problem
  • Ignoring site conditions: sun, shade, and moisture still matter
  • Using a cover that competes too strongly with crops: function should fit the bed
  • Leaving gaps of bare soil anyway: consistent coverage matters
  • Not managing young plantings while they establish: even good ground covers need early care

A Good Beginner Approach

If you are new to using ground covers, start by identifying one exposed area of the garden that repeatedly dries out, erodes, or weeds over.

Then choose a low-growing plant that:

  • suits that site's conditions
  • can cover the soil steadily
  • will stay manageable
  • adds some extra value if possible, such as pollinator support or food

That focused start often teaches more than trying to cover the entire garden at once.

Final Thoughts

The best ground covers for soil protection are the ones that keep the surface covered, hold the soil with roots, reduce moisture loss, and fit naturally into the garden system around them. Clover, strawberries, low herbs, native covers, and other suitable low-growing plants can all help, depending on the setting.

When chosen thoughtfully, ground covers do much more than fill space. They protect the soil, support life below the surface, and make the whole garden more stable and resilient over time.
